narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've built a solid academic foundation with your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical 3.21 average, and an LSAT score of 38, also higher than the usual 36.70. Your Year 1 school decile of 8 and Year 3 decile of 7 reflect consistent performance in the upper ranges compared to peers, and coming from a middle-income family puts you in a common bracket for applicants. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority—over 92%—of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 45.5%. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away to maintain their strong bar passage rates. The model's decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 school decile emerging as the single most influential, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by signaling solid late-stage academic strength. Close behind, your middle family income level worked against you, nudging the outlook toward potential failure more than other factors, possibly because it correlates with certain challenges seen in similar students' paths to the bar. Your LSAT score also played a major supportive role, pulling the prediction back toward success in a way that nearly balanced the income concern. Overall, while your metrics are competitive or better than average in several areas, the combination tipped the scale just enough due to that income influence outweighing some of your strengths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher, seeking scholarships or programs that support middle-income students like you, or bolstering your Year 3 record if possible through additional coursework. Building a narrative around your Hispanic background and resilience could also strengthen future applications at schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you're well-positioned to turn this around.
